(pl. **phenomena**), sometimes spelled **phaenomenon**, is an [observable](/wiki/Observable "Observable") [event](/wiki/Event_(philosophy) "Event (philosophy)").[[1]](#cite_note-Pensylvania_the_boiled_one_phenomenon-1) The term came into its modern [philosophical](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") usage through [Immanuel Kant](/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant"), who contrasted it with the [noumenon](/wiki/Noumenon "Noumenon"), which *cannot* be directly observed. Kant was heavily influenced by [Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz](/wiki/Gottfried_Wilhelm_Leibniz "Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz") in this part of his philosophy, in which phenomenon and noumenon serve as interrelated technical terms. Far predating this, the [ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_Philosophy "Ancient Greek Philosophy") [Pyrrhonist](/wiki/Pyrrhonism "Pyrrhonism") philosopher [Sextus Empiricus](/wiki/Sextus_Empiricus "Sextus Empiricus") also used *phenomenon* and *noumenon* as interrelated technical terms.

[Kant, Immanuel](/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant"). [1770] 2019. *[On the Form and Principles of the Sensible and Intelligible World](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Kant%27s_Inaugural_Dissertation_of_1770 "wikisource:Kant's Inaugural Dissertation of 1770")*, translated by W. J. Eckoff (1894). – via Wikisource.

["Mechanical Phenomenon"](http://www.audioenglish.net/dictionary/mechanical_phenomenon.htm). *AudioEnglish.org*. Tudorancea Media Network. [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20110723022250/http://www.audioenglish.net/dictionary/mechanical_phenomenon.htm) from the original on 23 July 2011. Retrieved 23 May 2011.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Phenomena](/w/index.php?title=Phenomena&redirect=no "Phenomena"))

Observable event

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Phenomenon "Special:EditPage/Phenomenon") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Phenomenon"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Phenomenon%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Phenomenon%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Phenomenon%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Phenomenon%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Phenomenon%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Phenomenon%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(August 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

"Phenomena" and "Phenomenal" redirect here. For other uses, see [Phenomena (disambiguation)](/wiki/Phenomena_(disambiguation) "Phenomena (disambiguation)") and [Phenomenal (disambiguation)](/wiki/Phenomenal_(disambiguation) "Phenomenal (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Streichholz.jpg/250px-Streichholz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Streichholz.jpg)

The combustion of a match is an observable occurrence, or event, and therefore a phenomenon.

A **phenomenon**  (pl. **phenomena**), sometimes spelled **phaenomenon**, is an [observable](/wiki/Observable "Observable") [event](/wiki/Event_(philosophy) "Event (philosophy)").[[1]](#cite_note-Pensylvania_the_boiled_one_phenomenon-1) The term came into its modern [philosophical](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") usage through [Immanuel Kant](/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant"), who contrasted it with the [noumenon](/wiki/Noumenon "Noumenon"), which *cannot* be directly observed. Kant was heavily influenced by [Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz](/wiki/Gottfried_Wilhelm_Leibniz "Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz") in this part of his philosophy, in which phenomenon and noumenon serve as interrelated technical terms. Far predating this, the [ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_Philosophy "Ancient Greek Philosophy") [Pyrrhonist](/wiki/Pyrrhonism "Pyrrhonism") philosopher [Sextus Empiricus](/wiki/Sextus_Empiricus "Sextus Empiricus") also used *phenomenon* and *noumenon* as interrelated technical terms.

## Common usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phenomenon&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Common usage")]

In popular usage, a *phenomenon* often refers to an extraordinary, unusual, or notable event. According to the *Dictionary of Visual Discourse*:[[2]](#cite_note-Dictionary-2)

> In ordinary language 'phenomenon/phenomena' refer to any noteworthy occurrence and investigation, typically an untoward or unusual event, person or fact that is of special significance or otherwise notable.

## Philosophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phenomenon&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Philosophy")]

See also: [Awareness](/wiki/Awareness "Awareness"), [Phenomenology (philosophy)](/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy) "Phenomenology (philosophy)"), [Essence](/wiki/Essence "Essence"), and [Condition of possibility](/wiki/Condition_of_possibility "Condition of possibility")

In modern philosophical use, the term *phenomena* means things as they are experienced through the senses and processed by the mind as distinct from things in and of themselves (*[noumena](/wiki/Noumenon "Noumenon")*). In his [inaugural dissertation](/wiki/Inaugural_dissertation "Inaugural dissertation"), titled *On the [Form](/wiki/Theory_of_forms "Theory of forms") and Principles of the Sensible and Intelligible World*, [Immanuel Kant](/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant") (1770) theorizes that the human mind is restricted to the logical world and thus can only interpret and understand occurrences according to their physical appearances. He wrote that humans could infer only as much as their senses allowed, but not experience the actual object itself.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Thus, the term *phenomenon* refers to any incident deserving of inquiry and investigation, especially processes and events which are particularly unusual or of distinctive importance.[[2]](#cite_note-Dictionary-2)

## Physiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phenomenon&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Physiology")]

See also: [Experience](/wiki/Experience "Experience"), [Intuition](/wiki/Intuition "Intuition"), and [Observation](/wiki/Observation "Observation")

## Science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phenomenon&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Science")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Space_Fire.jpg/250px-Space_Fire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Space_Fire.jpg)

A comparison between a candle flame on Earth (left) and in a [microgravity](/wiki/Microgravity "Microgravity") environment, such as that found on the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station") (right).  

See also: [Electrical phenomena](/wiki/Electrical_phenomena "Electrical phenomena") and [Optical phenomena](/wiki/Optical_phenomena "Optical phenomena")

The same *burning* phenomenon is [observed](/wiki/Observation "Observation"), but different flame *shape* and *colouring* phenomena are also observed.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Cloud_chamber_bionerd.jpg/250px-Cloud_chamber_bionerd.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cloud_chamber_bionerd.jpg)

[Cloud chamber](/wiki/Cloud_chamber "Cloud chamber") phenomena. Scientists use phenomena to refine some [hypotheses](/wiki/Hypotheses "Hypotheses") and sometimes to disprove a [theory](/wiki/Theory "Theory"). See also [animated version](/wiki/File:Cloud_chamber_ani_bionerd.gif "File:Cloud chamber ani bionerd.gif").

See also: [Phenomenalism](/wiki/Phenomenalism "Phenomenalism")

In scientific usage, a phenomenon is any event that is [observable](/wiki/Observable "Observable"), including the use of [instrumentation](/wiki/Instrumentation "Instrumentation") to observe, record, or compile data. Especially in [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"), the study of a phenomenon may be described as [measurements](/wiki/Measurement "Measurement") related to [matter](/wiki/Matter "Matter"), [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy"), or [time](/wiki/Time "Time"), such as [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton")'s observations of the [Moon's orbit](/wiki/Orbit_of_the_Moon "Orbit of the Moon") and of [gravity](/wiki/Universal_gravitation "Universal gravitation"); or [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei")'s observations of the motion of a [pendulum](/wiki/Pendulum "Pendulum").[[4]](#cite_note-Bernstein-4)

In [natural sciences](/wiki/Natural_science "Natural science"), a phenomenon is an observable happening or event. Often, this term is used without considering the causes of a particular event. Example of a physical phenomenon is an observable phenomenon of the lunar orbit or the phenomenon of oscillations of a pendulum.[[4]](#cite_note-Bernstein-4)

A mechanical phenomenon is a physical phenomenon associated with the [equilibrium](/wiki/Dynamic_equilibrium "Dynamic equilibrium") or [motion](/wiki/Motion_(physics) "Motion (physics)") of objects.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Some examples are [Newton's cradle](/wiki/Newton%27s_cradle "Newton's cradle"), [engines](/wiki/Engine "Engine"), and [double pendulums](/wiki/Double_pendulum "Double pendulum").

## Sociology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phenomenon&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Sociology")]

See also: [Phenomenology (sociology)](/wiki/Phenomenology_(sociology) "Phenomenology (sociology)") and [List of Internet phenomena](/wiki/List_of_Internet_phenomena "List of Internet phenomena")

Group phenomena concern the behavior of a particular group of individual entities, usually organisms and most especially people. The behavior of individuals often changes in a group setting in various ways, and a group may have its own behaviors not possible for an individual because of the [herd mentality](/wiki/Herd_mentality "Herd mentality").

[Social phenomena](/wiki/Social_phenomenon "Social phenomenon") apply especially to organisms and people in that subjective states are implicit in the term. Attitudes and events particular to a group may have effects beyond the group, and either be adapted by the larger society, or seen as aberrant, being punished or shunned.
